section.title: Assurance of Eternal Life
section.sectionRationale: This is the letter's conclusion and its pastoral goal: that those who believe in the Son may know they have eternal life. From that assurance flow confidence in prayer, three ringing certainties about the believer's security and the knowledge of the true God, and a closing charge to keep from every idol.
section.thesis: John writes so that believers may know they have eternal life in the Son, and from that certainty come confident prayer, assurance of being kept and belonging to God, true knowledge of the true God in Jesus Christ, and a final charge to keep themselves from idols.
section.frameNote: Four beats: written that you may know you have eternal life; confidence in prayer, even for a straying brother; the three great certainties, born of God, belonging to God, knowing the true God; and the closing charge, keep yourselves from idols.

units.label: That you may know
units.note: written so you may be sure

verses.text: I have written these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, so that you may {{oida|know}} that you have {{zoe|eternal life}}.
And this is the {{parresia|confidence}} that we have before Him: If we ask anything according to His will, He hears us.
And if we know that He hears us in whatever we ask, we know that we already possess what we have asked of Him.

If anyone sees his brother committing a sin not leading to death, he should ask God, who will give life to those who commit this kind of sin. There is a {{hamartia|sin}} that leads to death; I am not saying he should ask regarding that sin.
All unrighteousness is sin, yet there is sin that does not lead to death.

units.label: What we know
units.note: born of God, belonging to God, knowing the true God

verses.text: We know that anyone born of God does not keep on sinning; the One who was born of God protects him, and the evil one cannot touch him.
We know that we are of God, and that the whole world is under the power of the evil one.

And we know that the Son of God has come and has given us understanding, so that we may know Him who is {{alethinos|true}}; and we are in Him who is true, in His Son Jesus Christ. He is the true God and eternal life.

oida.gloss: To know, and to know with settled certainty. John writes so that believers may know they have eternal life. This is not a hopeful guess but assured knowledge, resting on God's testimony about His Son. The word rings through the closing verses: we know, we know, we know.

zoe.gloss: Life, eternal life, the very life of God given in Christ. John's aim is that believers may know they have this life now, a present possession secured in the Son and reaching into eternity.

parresia.gloss: Confidence, boldness, freedom of speech before another. It is the settled assurance with which the child of God comes to the Father in prayer, certain of a hearing when the request accords with His will. The same word marked the believer's confidence at the day of judgment earlier in the letter.

hamartia.gloss: Sin, a missing of the mark, offence against God and His law. John distinguishes a sin that leads to death from sin that does not, and calls all unrighteousness sin. The sin that leads to death is best understood as the settled, unrepentant rejection of Christ, not a single act.

alethinos.gloss: True, real, genuine, as opposed to the false or counterfeit. The Son has come and given understanding to know the One who is true; believers are in Him who is true, in His Son Jesus Christ, who is the true God and eternal life. This word sets the real God against the idols of the closing verse.

eidolon.gloss: An idol, an image of a false god, and by extension anything put in God's place. John's closing charge is to keep from idols, guarding the true knowledge of the true God just given. Any rival to Christ, however refined, is an idol to be refused.

basic.context: John tells his readers plainly why he wrote this letter: so that you who believe in the name of the Son of God may know that you have eternal life. Assurance is the goal. From that certainty flows confidence in prayer: when we ask according to God's will, He hears us, and we can even pray for a straying brother. John then piles up three great certainties: those born of God are kept from a life of sin and from the grip of the evil one; we belong to God while the whole world lies under the evil one; and the Son has come and given us understanding to know the true God. The letter ends with a sharp, tender charge: little children, keep yourselves from idols.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the classic assurance passage. John does not want believers wracked by doubt; he wrote so they may know. That knowledge is not arrogance or mere feeling but rests on God's testimony about His Son. The hard verse about the sin that leads to death has puzzled many; read in the light of the whole letter, it points to the settled rejection of Christ by those who walked away, not a checklist of unforgivable acts. The final warning against idols is not a change of subject but the flip side of knowing the true God.
basic.originalLanguages: John's aim is that we may know, oida, with certainty that we have life, zoe. That certainty gives confidence, parresia, in prayer. He weighs sin, hamartia, distinguishing the sin that leads to death from the sin that does not. And he ends by setting the true, alethinos, God against every idol, eidolon, to be refused.
basic.typology: The closing charge against idols gathers up the whole Bible's war on false gods, from the golden calf to the prophets' pleas, now focused on holding to the true God revealed in His Son. The confident access to God in prayer answers the old distance, when only the priest could draw near, opened now to every child of God.
basic.christ: The Son of God stands at the centre of assurance. We know we have eternal life because we believe in His name, and He is the One who has come and given us understanding to know the true God. Indeed John says of Him, He is the true God and eternal life, a clear witness to Christ's deity. To be in the Son is to be in the true One; to leave Him for any idol is to leave life itself.
basic.god: God hears the prayers of His children, giving what accords with His will. He keeps those born of Him so the evil one cannot hold them. And He is the true God, over against every idol, made known in His Son. The letter's God is faithful, near, and real, worthy of all trust and of undivided allegiance.
basic.crossReferences: John 20:31 states the same purpose, that believing you may have life in His name. Mark 11:24 speaks of confidence in prayer. Hebrews 4:16 invites us to approach the throne of grace with boldness. And the first commandment, Exodus 20:3, stands behind the charge to keep from idols.
basic.oneStory: The whole story moves toward a people who know their God, belong to Him, and worship Him alone. Here John gives that people assurance: you may know you have eternal life in the Son. He is the true God, and the ancient call away from idols to the living God finds its rest in Him. The letter closes where the Bible is heading, in sure fellowship with the true God through His Son.

context.literary: The conclusion of the letter, marked by the repeated 'we know'. John states his purpose, so that you may know that you have eternal life, then draws out the confidence, the certainties, and the closing charge. It gathers the letter's tests of belief, obedience, and love into settled assurance.
context.historical: Written to believers unsettled by teachers who had left the fellowship claiming superior knowledge, John reassures those who held to Christ that they, not the departed, possess eternal life. In a world full of idols, literal and refined, the final charge warned a young church to hold to the true God against every counterfeit.
context.canonical: The passage is a fountainhead for the doctrine of assurance, that believers may know they are saved, and a strong witness to the deity of Christ, who is called the true God and eternal life. Its closing warning against idols joins the whole canon's insistence on worshipping the one true God alone.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure those who believe in the Son that they have eternal life, to give them confidence in prayer, to fix their certainty on what God has done in His Son, and to charge them to guard that knowledge by keeping from every idol.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what believers may know, that they have life, that God hears them, that they are kept and belong to Him, and it prescribes the responses: pray with confidence, pray for a straying brother, and keep yourselves from idols. The certainties ground the commands.

debates.question: What is the sin that leads to death (verses 16 and 17)?

views.label: The settled, unrepentant rejection of Christ
views.case: The strongest reading, in light of the whole letter, is that the sin leading to death is not a single act but a hardened, final turning from Christ, like the antichrists who left the fellowship, 1 John 2:19. Such apostasy, persisted in, ends in death because it refuses the only source of life. John does not forbid praying for such a person, but he does not command it, since the sin is defined by rejecting the Son in whom life is found.

views.label: A specific grievous act of sin
views.case: Some identify it with a particular sin, such as murder or blasphemy; but John does not name an act, and the letter frames life and death around having or not having the Son.

views.label: Physical death as divine discipline
views.case: Others link it to bodily death under God's discipline, as in 1 Corinthians 11:30; possible, but the letter's contrast is between spiritual life in the Son and death apart from Him.

debates.bearing: The strongest reading is that the sin that leads to death is the settled rejection of Christ, the only source of life, rather than a particular deed. This keeps assurance intact for the struggling believer, whose ongoing repentance shows life, while taking seriously the deadly end of turning finally from the Son.

debates.question: Can a believer truly know they have eternal life?

views.label: Yes, assurance is possible and intended
views.case: The strongest reading is that God means His children to be sure. John says outright that he wrote so that you may know that you have eternal life. That assurance rests on God's objective testimony about His Son and the gift of life in Him, confirmed inwardly by the Spirit and outwardly by the fruit of faith, obedience, and love that the letter has described. It is confidence in God's work, not in our own.

views.label: Assurance is presumption; no one can be sure
views.case: Some hold that claiming to know is arrogant and that believers should remain uncertain; but this contradicts John's stated purpose, that you may know, and treats God's clear testimony as insufficient.

views.label: Assurance rests on inner feelings
views.case: Others ground assurance in emotional experience; but feelings rise and fall, while John grounds knowing in God's testimony about His Son and the evidences of a changed life.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading is that believers may and should know they have eternal life, resting on God's testimony about His Son and confirmed by the Spirit and the fruit of faith and love. Assurance is neither presumption nor mere feeling but confidence in what God has done in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The closing verses ring with certainty. John writes so that we may oida, know beyond doubt, that we have zoe, eternal life, and that knowledge yields parresia, confident, free access to God in prayer. He weighs hamartia, sin, distinguishing the sin that leads to death, best read as the settled rejection of Christ, from the sin that does not. Three times he says 'we know', and the last of these lands on the alethinos, the true God, whom the Son has come to make known. So the letter ends where knowledge of the true God demands: keep yourselves from every eidolon, every idol that would take His place.

typology.type: Confident access to God in prayer
typology.fulfillment: The old covenant distance, when only the priest drew near on set terms, opened so that every child of God comes with boldness through the Son.

typology.type: The charge to keep from idols
typology.fulfillment: The first commandment and the prophets' long war on idolatry, brought to rest in holding to the true God revealed in Jesus Christ.

study.christ: Christ is the ground and object of every certainty here. Believers know they have eternal life because they believe in the name of the Son of God. The Son has come and given understanding to know the true God, and John says of Him plainly, He is the true God and eternal life, a direct affirmation of Christ's deity. To be in the Son is to be in the true One and to have life; to turn from Him to an idol is to lose both.
study.god: God is the faithful Father who hears His children when they ask according to His will, and who keeps those born of Him so the evil one cannot touch them. He is the true God, real and living, over against the idols that clutter the world. Made known in His Son, He gives eternal life and invites His people into confident, unafraid fellowship with Himself.

crossReferences.note: Written that you may believe and have life in His name, the same purpose as 5:13.
Those who left us were never truly of us, the background to the sin that leads to death.
Approach the throne of grace with confidence, matching the boldness John describes in prayer.
You shall have no other gods before Me, the commandment behind the closing charge to keep from idols.

study.oneStory: Scripture moves toward a people who know the true God, belong to Him, and worship Him alone through His Son. John's letter closes on exactly this: you may know you have eternal life, God hears you, He keeps you, and He is the true God made known in Jesus Christ. The ancient summons out of idolatry into fellowship with the living God finds its rest in the Son, in whom the whole story arrives at assurance and home.

sermon.title: Christian Certainties
sermon.why: Begg preaches this closing section, 1 John 5:13-21, on the certainty that believers have eternal life, confidence in prayer, God's keeping of His own, and the final charge to keep from idols. A clear, orthodox handling of the letter's conclusion.

quiz.q: Why did John write this letter, according to verse 13?

options.text: So that those who believe in the name of the Son of God may know that they have eternal life.
options.note: Assurance is the stated goal of the letter.

options.text: So that believers would grow in knowledge and love over time.
options.note: Growth is a fruit, but John's stated aim here is assured knowledge of eternal life.

options.text: So that believers might hope they are saved without ever being sure.
options.note: John wrote so that you may know, not merely hope.

options.text: So that readers would earn eternal life by obeying his instructions.
options.note: Eternal life is God's gift in the Son, not a wage earned by obedience.

quiz.q: What is the confidence believers have in prayer (verses 14 and 15)?

options.text: That when we ask anything according to God's will, He hears us.
options.note: Confidence is anchored to asking according to His will.

options.text: That God hears our prayers and works out what is good, in His way and time.
options.note: True and pastoral, but John's specific point is asking according to His will.

options.text: That God grants whatever we ask if we believe hard enough.
options.note: The measure is God's will, not the intensity of our belief.

options.text: That God gives us whatever we want with no regard to His will.
options.note: John ties the promise directly to praying according to His will.

quiz.q: How is the sin that leads to death best understood (verses 16 and 17)?

options.text: As the settled, unrepentant rejection of Christ, the only source of life.
options.note: Read with the whole letter, it is a hardened turning from the Son, not one act.

options.text: As bodily death brought about by God's discipline.
options.note: Some read it this way, but the letter frames life and death around having the Son.

options.text: As one particular act, such as murder or blasphemy.
options.note: John names no specific act; the issue is rejecting the Son in whom life is found.

options.text: As any sin at all, so that a single sin cuts a believer off from life.
options.note: John says all unrighteousness is sin, yet distinguishes sin that does not lead to death.

quiz.q: What does John affirm about Jesus Christ in verse 20?

options.text: He is the true God and eternal life, in whom believers know the One who is true.
options.note: A direct affirmation of the Son's deity.

options.text: He is the Son through whom God gives understanding of Himself.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but John goes further and calls the Son the true God and eternal life.

options.text: He is a true teacher who points us to God but is not Himself God.
options.note: John says more than this; He is the true God.

options.text: He is one true way to God among several.
options.note: John locates life in the Son alone, who is the true God.

quiz.q: What is the closing charge of the letter (verse 21)?

options.text: Little children, keep yourselves from idols.
options.note: The letter ends by guarding the true knowledge of the true God against every rival.

options.text: Love one another, for that sums up the whole letter.
options.note: A true and central command of the letter, but the actual closing charge is to keep from idols.

options.text: Return to keeping the ceremonial law.
options.note: John's concern is undivided worship of the true God, not a return to ceremonial law.

options.text: Feel free to add other loyalties alongside God.
options.note: This is the opposite of the charge to keep from idols.

questions: John wrote so that you may know you have eternal life. Do you live with that settled assurance, or with nagging doubt, and what does John say your assurance should rest on?
Confidence in prayer means asking according to God's will. How would your prayers change if your first concern were His will rather than your own preferences?
The sin that leads to death is the settled rejection of Christ. How does that truth both sober you about turning from Him and comfort you when you stumble and repent?
John calls Jesus the true God and eternal life. What difference does it make to your worship and trust that the Son is fully God and is Himself your life?
The letter ends, keep yourselves from idols. What rivals to God, subtle or obvious, most tempt to take His place in your heart right now?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts add 'and that you may believe in the name of the Son of God', as the BSB footnote notes; either way the verse states John's purpose that believers may know they have eternal life.

'The sin that leads to death' is much debated; in light of the whole letter it most likely refers to the settled, unrepentant rejection of Christ rather than a particular act. John does not forbid praying for such a person but does not command it.

The clause 'the One who was born of God protects him' can also be rendered 'he who was born of God keeps himself', as the BSB footnote notes; the sense is that the believer is kept safe so the evil one cannot hold him.
'He is the true God and eternal life' most naturally refers to Jesus Christ, the nearest antecedent, making this one of the New Testament's clear affirmations of the deity of the Son.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 5:13-21, reaching the end of the letter at 5:21. The em dash in the BSB at verse 20 was rendered with a comma to match house style; the wording is otherwise unchanged. Textual and interpretive notes at 5:13, 5:16, 5:18, and 5:20 follow the BSB footnotes and the letter's own framing.
